subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear overall structure with a defined introduction, body and conclusion
Effective use of tables (e.g. Table 7 and Table 2) to support the narrative
Concise presentation of the TSP bounds inequality
weaknesses: Long unbroken and duplicated tables reduce conciseness
Some graphics are misplaced or lack adequate captions (e.g. Figure 1)
Anecdotal remarks and garbled material (Table 8) interrupt logical flow
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and well organised, with clear logical development in most sections (band 3 in coherence and organisation) but contains unnecessary or mis-formatted material and lacks seamless flow (band 2 in conciseness). Overall this warrants a middleâband performance at level 2 out of 4.

strengths: Good variety of representations (maps, GeoGebra screenshots, adjacency matrices)
Step-by-step algorithm tables (e.g. Deleted Vertex Algorithm) are clearly outlined
weaknesses: Numerous mis-rendered symbols (Î©, Unicode errors) and unexplained notation hinder clarity
Key tables (Table 6 and Table 8) are corrupted and unreadable
Explanatory bridges between representations are often missing or incomplete
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While there is some use of multiple representations, pervasive notation errors and unreadable tables severely disrupt clarity and consistency (band 1 overall). This limits the mathematical communication to the lowest passing level, meriting a score of 1 out of 4.

strengths: Investigation is grounded in the studentâs personal pizza-delivery experience
Evidence of independent thought in choosing and measuring real-world routes
Creative initiative in introducing a scale factor and testing deleted-vertex cases
weaknesses: Algorithms themselves follow standard templates rather than novel adaptations
Personal narrative could be more tightly integrated into the mathematical development
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows significant independent thinking, a clear personal approach and notable creativity (all band 2 descriptors), but does not reach the outstanding originality required for band 3. Thus it earns 2 out of 3.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on model limitations (one-way streets, straight-line distances)
Basic critique of assumptions and suggestions for refinement (multiple hubs, real-time data)
Connections drawn between TSP bounds and delivery-cost understanding
weaknesses: Reflection remains at a surface level without quantitative error analysis
Limited depth of critical analysis of scaling and rounding impacts
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides meaningful reflection and some critique (band 2) with clear connections to their understanding, but does not demonstrate the depth and critical rigour required for band 3. Score: 2 out of 3.

strengths: Appropriate use of HL-level mathematics: graph theory, adjacency matrices and TSP bounds
Correct application of triangle inequalities and MST-based lower bound
Contextual relevance of mathematical techniques to the delivery-route problem
weaknesses: Arithmetic and rounding inconsistencies in distance scaling
Transcription errors and garbled tables introduce uncertainty
Misinterpretation between MST and TSP tour in places
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics applied is largely at course level and effective (band 4 in relevance/use), with mostly correct conceptual understanding but several execution errors (bands 3 in accuracy and understanding). On balance this merits a 3 out of 6 for Use of Mathematics.

strandSelections.reasoning: Selecting personal neighbourhood data, measuring distances on foot and deciding to compare two TSP bounds show significant independent thought beyond textbook imitation, though not outstanding originality.

The student demonstrates good conceptual grasp of position vectors, adjacency matrices and bounding algorithms, yet occasional misinterpretations (e.g. confusing MST with TSP tour) prevent a âthorough and rigorousâ judgement.

Distance calculations, scale conversion and algorithmic totals are largely plausible, but transcription errors and garbled tables introduce uncertainty.  Accuracy is therefore âmostly correctâ with minor to moderate errors.

Most material is relevant to solving the delivery-route problem, but there are sizeable portions of duplicated or garbled tables (Table 8) and anecdotal remarks that do not advance the mathematics.  Trimming these would improve concision; hence the strand sits at âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ.

Standard notation for vectors, magnitudes, matrices and inequalities is attempted and generally recognisable.  Nonetheless, several symbols are mis-rendered (Î©, random Unicode) and subscripts appear where numbers should be, so accuracy and consistency are only partial.  This aligns with the middle band.

Explanations are clear in many sections (e.g. scale-factor derivation) but inconsistent in others due to typographical noise and unexplained symbols.  Overall clarity is âmostlyâ but not fully consistent, fitting band 2.

Graph theory, adjacency matrices, TSP bounds and vector geometry are all HL-appropriate and are applied purposefully to the context.  Despite some execution flaws, the mathematics is clearly relevant and generally effective, suiting band 4.

The student uses maps, GeoGebra screenshots, weighted graphs, adjacency matrices and step-by-step algorithm tables.  These multiple representations support comprehension even though some are cluttered.  The variety and generally effective linking merit the âeffective useâ band.

The student reflects on how one-way streets, single-store assumption and straight-line distances limit realism, and suggests multiple hubs and real-time data as refinements.  This is meaningful reflection, though not deeply critical.

Introducing a scale factor, manually walking some routes, and testing various deleted-vertex cases demonstrate creativity and initiative, yet the algorithms themselves follow standard templates, so the strand earns the middle band.

The report follows a recognizable sequence â rationale, data collection, modelling, algorithms, results and conclusion.  Each part builds on the previous one, so the reader can see how the initial question leads to graph construction and on to TSP bounds.  Occasional digressions (e.g. repeated explanations of assumptions) interrupt the flow, yet the overall line of argument is preserved.  This places the work comfortably in the âcoherent with clear logical developmentâ band rather than the exemplary band.

There is analysis of upper vs lower bounds and of assumption validity, but the critique remains surface-level (no quantitative error analysis of scaling, for example).  Thus the work shows âsome critiqueâ rather than âdeepâ.

Links between mathematical results and delivery-cost understanding are drawn (e.g., estimating number of drivers from distance range).  These connections enhance understanding but are brief, placing the work in band 2.

Headings, numbered tables / figures and a beginning-middle-end structure show deliberate organisation.  The introduction frames the aim, the body delivers the mathematics, and a short conclusion wraps up findings.  However, long unbroken tables and some misplaced graphics reduce smoothness, so the work is well-organised but not âexceptionallyâ seamless.

The investigation is clearly rooted in the studentâs Sunday pizza experience; this personal context drives decisions on node choice and assumptions, giving a discernible personal voice that propels the work.

annotations.comment: When summing the minimum weights (1290+1121) for the sample route, explicitly show the arithmetic step to reinforce mathematical communication.
Table 7 is neatly presented and supports the narrative by consolidating least distances in a clear adjacency format.
Figure 1 is referenced in the text but neither its role nor its labels are fully explained. Improve the caption to describe how the map informs node selection.
The rationale provides context but lacks a clear, focused research question. Consider explicitly stating the question you aim to answer to guide the exploration and improve coherence.
The aim section is overly verbose and contains expectations without clear measurable objectives. Refine it to a concise statement of intent, specifying the methods and metrics you will use.
The assumptions listed to construct the graph are not explicitly numbered or justified. Clarify each assumptionâs necessity and potential impact on validity.
When introducing vector subtraction, include the general formula $$\overrightarrow{XY} = \overrightarrow{Y} - \overrightarrow{X}$$ before applying to specific nodes.
Table 2 is well formatted and clearly communicates each position vector relative to the origin, aiding comprehension.
The scaling factor calculation uses 881 m but earlier you measured 901 m. Verify consistency in measured distances to avoid propagating a systematic error.
The real-world distances in Table 3 are rounded to 3 s.f. but the resulting values (e.g., 854 m) do not match exact products of magnitude and scale factor. Check your arithmetic and rounding.
The text explains using direct distances as a basis for adjacent edges but doesnât show how these are implemented in the least-distance matrix. Add a brief bridging paragraph.
The reflection on model limitations and road restrictions is insightful, demonstrating thoughtful consideration of real-world constraints.
In the subtraction for $$\overrightarrow{BC}$$ you quote 40.1488 m. After scaling, 40.1488Ã41.4393â1664 m, not 1667 m. Minor arithmetical precision needs review.
The demonstration of the triangle inequalities is correct and effectively justifies selection of the shortest edges.
The calculated upper bound sum text contains a mismatch: 854+1667+â¦+10789 should yield 45702 m, not 51682 m as later stated. Correct the summary to maintain accuracy.
Many entries in the weighted adjacency matrix use symbols like âÎ©â without explanation, hindering clarity. Replace with explicit âno connectionâ or âââ along with a legend.
The explanation of transforming the incomplete matrix into a complete one via least-distance comparisons is concise and clearly prepares for the TSP table.
The description of the Nearest Neighbor Algorithm outlines steps but could be enhanced by stating how ties are broken or visited-node restrictions handled.
Table 8 (Nearest Neighbor steps) appears heavily corrupted and unreadable. Reconstruct it using a simple list or table format to communicate the algorithmic choices.
Table 6 (sample deletion) is severely corrupted, making it impossible to follow the MST construction. Redraft this example cleanly with correct labels and values.
The inequality \(30938\le m\le45703\) is a clear and concise presentation of the TSP bounds, nicely concluding the mathematical journey.
The overview of the Deleted Vertex Algorithm clearly outlines each step, demonstrating good mathematical communication.
The lower bound calculation in text correctly applies MST weight and two smallest edges, giving 27263 m. This shows effective use of graph theory methods.
The reference list lacks consistent formatting (e.g., missing publication details). Follow a uniform citation style to enhance presentation.
The conclusion restates results but would benefit from deeper reflection on how assumptions might quantitatively impact the interval width.
